description:
Authors: Banatt, E. and Cheng, J. Y. and Vaidyanath, S. and Hwu, T.
Title: WILT: A multi-turn, memorization-robust inductive logic benchmark for LLMs
Year: 2024
URL: URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10998
reason: Paper confirmed via arXiv ID (2410.10998), consistent title/authors/year across sources, hosted on arXiv, presented at NeurIPS 2024 workshop, and cited externally. Semantic Scholar's lack of indexing is not sufficient to质疑 authenticity given multiple corroborating sources.
prompt_variant: You are a critical academic research verifier. Your task: determine if a bibliog...
_search_results:
**Yes, the paper exists.**

**Exact title:** WILT: A Multi-Turn, Memorization-Robust Inductive Logic Benchmark for LLMs[1][3][4][7].

**Authors:** Eryk Banatt, Jonathan Cheng, Skanda Vaidyanath, Tiffany Hwu[3][4][7] (Note: Query lists "Banatt E. Cheng J.Y. Vaidyanath S. Hwu T.", which matches but uses initials; full names confirmed in sources).

**Venue:** arXiv preprint[4][7].

**Year:** 2024[1][3][4][7].

**DOI/arXiv ID:** arXiv:2410.10998[1][4][7] (full versions at https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10998 and https://arxiv.org/html/2410.10998v1)[1][4].

Additional details: Presented as a poster at the MATH-AI Workshop (NeurIPS 2024)[3]; code available on GitHub[6]; cited 11 times as of latest data[7].
Semantic Scholar: no matching paper found.

**arXiv:2410.10998 introduces the WILT (Wason Inductive Logic Test) benchmark, a multi-turn, memorization-resistant test for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on inductive reasoning.[1][2]**

WILT draws from the Wason 2-4-6 task, where models propose test cases (e.g., (2,4,6)), receive True/False feedback, and iteratively refine hypotheses to identify a hidden rule (e.g., \(x < y < z\)).[2] It emphasizes multi-turn evidence gathering and hypothesis narrowing, resisting memorization through procedural generation.[1][2]

Key performance results on WILT (50 examples) include:

| Model                  | Exact Matches (/50) | Perfect Rules (/50) | Avg. Score (%) | Avg. Steps |
|------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------|------------|
| Mistral Large 2       | 11                  | 5                   | 26.56         | 2.84      |[2]
| GPT-4o (2024-08-06)   | 9                   | 6                   | 15.26         | 0.52      |[2]
| GPT-4o-mini           | 6                   | 2                   | 20.36         | 1.08      |[2]
| Gemini 1.5 Pro        | 5                   | 6                   | 16.78         | 0.82      |[2]
| Gemini 1.5 Flash      | 5                   | 6                   | 16.5          | 2.46      |[2]

Claude 3.5 Sonnet outperforms models like o1-mini and o1-preview on WILT, despite their strength in single-turn tasks; top models reach only 28% accuracy, revealing LLM gaps in complex multi-turn reasoning.[2] WILT is referenced in other works like MIR-Bench as a 50-example inductive reasoning benchmark with ~30 turns.[4]
